step2_rag_chunks: Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

- If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
- Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Therapist: You're feeling a bit anxious about saying no to your friends and feeling like they might not understand. Would it help to have someone in your life who knows about your quit plan and can offer you support and encouragement in tough situations?

Patient: My boyfriend's really understanding, he's always trying to help me. But even with him, it's hard to ask for help when I'm feeling overwhelmed. I feel like I'm already a burden to him with my anxiety.

Therapist: Considering your anxiety often gets the better of you when you're feeling overwhelmed, would it be helpful to have a simple phrase or word that you can say to your boyfriend, like "I need a minute," to signal to him that you're feeling overwhelmed and need some space?
Patient: That could work, I guess. But what if he thinks I'm just being anxious again and tries to talk me out of it? I don't want to be that person all the time.

Therapist: You're feeling a bit uncertain about how your boyfriend will react if you ask for space. Would it help to practice saying that simple phrase, "I need a minute," with me in this session, so you can feel more comfortable using it in real-life situations?

step2_rag_chunks: When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.
Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.

A craving is not a command â€” it is a wave. It builds, peaks, and fades, usually within 3 to 5 minutes. Urge surfing means observing the craving without acting on it â€” noticing where you feel it in your body, watching it get stronger, and then watching it pass. Each time you surf an urge without smoking, it gets slightly easier the next time.

step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
